coredata.dc:title: A novel CRISPR/Cas14a system integrated with 2D porphyrin metal-organic framework for microcystin-LR determination through a homogeneous competitive reaction

coredata.dc:description: Selective and sensitive detection of microcystin-LR (MC-LR) is of vital importance because of its high toxicity and broad distribution. Herein, a novel and versatile fluorescence sensor (Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor) was developed by combining the CRISPR/Cas14a system with a 2D porphyrin metal-organic framework nanosheets (2D-pMOFs) for MC-LR determination. The designed CRISPR/Cas14a system was activated by the unbound complementary DNA (cDNA), which was positively correlated with MC-LR concentration. Furthermore, the activated Cas14a protein was utilized to indiscriminately cleave the FAM-labeled single-stranded DNA (ssDNA-FAM), which was pre-absorbed on Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets. Because of the desorption of the cleaved ssDNA-FAM, the pre-quenched fluorescence signal was recovered. Owing to the excellent performance in quantifying cDNA using this Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.12 nM, this Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor was able to detect MC-LR in a range from 50 pg/mL to 1 μg/mL with the LOD of 19 pg/mL. This work not only provided a new insight for the exploration of fluorescence sensors based on 2D-pMOFs coupled with CRISPR/Cas14a, but also, demonstrated its universality in both nucleic acid and non-nucleic acid targets determination.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Selective and sensitive detection of microcystin-LR (MC-LR) is of vital importance because of its high toxicity and broad distribution. Herein, a novel and versatile fluorescence sensor (Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor) was developed by combining the CRISPR/Cas14a system with a 2D porphyrin metal-organic framework nanosheets (2D-pMOFs) for MC-LR determination. The designed CRISPR/Cas14a system was activated by the unbound complementary DNA (cDNA), which was positively correlated with MC-LR concentration. Furthermore, the activated Cas14a protein was utilized to indiscriminately cleave the FAM-labeled single-stranded DNA (ssDNA-FAM), which was pre-absorbed on Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets. Because of the desorption of the cleaved ssDNA-FAM, the pre-quenched fluorescence signal was recovered. Owing to the excellent performance in quantifying cDNA using this Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.12 nM, this Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor was able to detect MC-LR in a range from 50 pg/mL to 1 μg/mL with the LOD of 19 pg/mL. This work not only provided a new insight for the exploration of fluorescence sensors based on 2D-pMOFs coupled with CRISPR/Cas14a, but also, demonstrated its universality in both nucleic acid and non-nucleic acid targets determination.

ce:section.ce:para: Microcystin-LR (MC-LR), a cyclic peptide hepatotoxin with high physical and chemical stability, is one of the most common and toxic hepatotoxins released by cyanobacteria (Yang et al., 2020; Ma et al., 2015). MC-LR is proved to be harmful to human health and aquatic ecosystems as a result of its high toxicity to the liver, kidneys, brain, and reproductive system (Zhan et al., 2020). Due to the frequent cyanobacteria blooms, an urgent requirement is put forward for the accurate and sensitive detection of MC-LR. Because the lower amount of MC-LR is detected during its release from cyanobacteria, the protective measures can be taken earlier to reduce its hazardous impact on humans and aquatic ecosystems (Yu et al., 2016). Currently, the main MC-LR analytical techniques are the enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), and liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS), which showed disadvantages in the requirement of sophisticated instruments, time-consuming pre-treatment, and tedious operation (Wang et al., 2021). In 2012, Ng et al. reported a specific DNA aptamer to MC-LR with high affinity (Kd= 50 ± 12 nM), this spectacular progress provided a probability to transduce the aptamer-target recognition event into readable signals in constructing biosensors (Ng et al., 2012). Compared to traditional detection methods, such as ELISA, HPLC and LC-MS, the biosensors exhibited superiority in selectivity, sensitivity as well as portability for MC-LR determination (Wu et al., 2020). For example, Li and co-workers reported an aptamer-based colorimetric sensor for the determination of environmental MC-LR with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.37 ng/mL (Li et al., 2016). Li and co-workers developed a fluorescence and surface-enhanced raman spectroscopic dual-modal aptasensor for MC-LR detection with a LOD of 0.50 ng/mL (Li et al., 2020). However, we notice that, even though a great effort has been provided to develop biosensors for MC-LR detection, the use of appropriate signal amplification strategies with simple detection steps is still the main challenge in achieving sensitive detection in the existed applications (Suo et al., 2021; Kordasht et al., 2020).

Currently, CRISPR/Cas systems, discovered as RNA-guided adaptive immune systems in bacteria, attracted a lot of attention in the development of new analytical tools for ultra-sensitive detection of key biomarkers (Horvath and Barrangou, 2010; Burgess, 2013). The CRISPR class 2 systems (Cas12, Cas13, and Cas 14a) showed attractive analytical features owning to their ability to precisely recognize target DNA/RNA, followed by cis and trans cleavage of DNA/RNA, which would cause a signal amplification thereby benefit for sensitive detection (Zhang et al., 2021). For example, the DETECTR and SHERLOCK are the two representative works using CRISPR/Cas-based biosensing technologies (Chen et al., 2018; Kellner et al., 2019). Most recently, the Cas14a, as the smallest Cas protein so far, was reported by Jennifer’s group, which is capable of targeting and cleaving any single-stranded DNAs (ssDNAs) with random sequences (Harrington et al., 2018). This discovery broadens its applications in analytical perspective, considering the protospacer adjacent motif (PAM) is not always available (Savage, 2019). Interesting to notice that Cas14a possesses higher fidelity to ssDNA sequences compared with that of Cas12a (Chen et al., 2018; Harrington et al., 2018), for instance, two Cas14a-mediated nucleic acid detection platforms for bacterial nucleic acid diagnosis were developed with an extremely low LOD of 1 aM (Ge et al., 2021; Song et al., 2021). These progress inspired us that the Cas14-based biosensing technologies could be designed to sensitive detect MC-LR after introducing functional DNA molecules such as aptamers.

However, the effectively and sufficiently transfer and further amplify the CRISPR/Cas system signal is a key point in the construction of these types of sensors. In the past decade, a number of signal transduction methods have been developed in CRISPR/Cas based biosensors, such as fluorescence spectroscopy (Li et al., 2021), colorimetry (Hu et al., 2020), electrochemistry (Dai et al., 2019), microfluidics (Ramachandran et al., 2020), and lateral flow strips (Xiong et al., 2021). Among them, fluorescence transduction, which mainly relies on fluorescence recovery of quencher-labeled ssDNA fluorescence reporter after being cleaved by the Cas protein, is considered as the most classic, simple, and sensitive method (Shen et al., 2020). However, a relatively high fluorescence background signal was observed frequently due to the limited quenching efficiency of traditional quenchers (Sheng et al., 2021). Alternatively, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are considered as good fluorescence quenchers for CRISPR/Cas-based biosensors due to their superior fluorescence quenching ability and good bio-compatibility (Chen et al., 2021). Many kinds of MOFs have been proved to be able to quench fluorescence, including, but not limited to ZIF-8, MIL-101, HKUST-1, UiO-66, Cu(H2dtoa) (Meng et al., 2020; Osman et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2020). These MOFs can absorb ssDNA through hydrogen bonding, π-π stacking and/or electrostatic interactions. Thus, the fluorescence quenching of the fluorophore-labeled ssDNA can be achieved through photo-induced electron transfer (PET) and/or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process. However, an insufficient fluorescence quenching efficiency and/or a long quenching time were found among these developed MOFs. 2D porphyrin metal-organic framework nanosheets (2D-pMOFs) absorb light in a broad range, indicating it can efficiently quench various fluorescence donors (Zhao et al., 2015). And the ssDNA can be strongly adsorbed on the porphyrin ligand owing to its conjugated π-electron system. Therefore, the fluorophore-labeled ssDNA can be easily adsorbed to the basal surface of 2D-pMOFs without chemical reactions, resulting in fluorescence quenching, which makes it more suitable for fluorescence sensing (Wang et al., 2019).

Herein, a novel CRISPR/Cas14a-based fluorescence sensor was developed by integrating Cas14a with 2D-pMOFs (Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor). For the first time, the MC-LR was detected using the Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor. Owing to the high affinity of MC-LR to MC-LR aptamer, the MC-LR competitively bind to the MC-LR aptamer and occupy the binding sites for the complementary DNA (cDNA). Thus, the amount of unbound cDNA in the solution phase, which was designed as an activator to trigger the collateral cleavage activity of Cas14a, was proportional to the MC-LR concentrations. The following indiscriminately cleavage of FAM-labeled ssDNA (ssDNA-FAM), which was pre-adsorbed on the surface of 2D-pMOFs, was accomplished by the activated Cas14a. The cleaved DNA fragments were easily desorbed from the 2D-pMOFs (He et al., 2010; Huang and Liu, 2018; Sheng et al., 2021), and emitted fluorescence signals. In this newly designed sensor, the specificity of the aptamer and high fidelity of Cas14a ensured the accuracy of MC-LR detection, the trans cleavage activity of Cas14a amplified the signal, while the excellent fluorescence quenching and ssDNA absorption ability of 2D-pMOFs ensured the signal output. Equally important is that this method expanded the application of the CRISPR/Cas14 system for the detection of non-nucleic acid targets.

Cas14a was expressed and purified by Novoprotein Scientific Inc. (Shanghai, China) according to reported methods (Harrington et al., 2018), the results of expression and purification were shown in Fig. S1. sgRNA was transcribed in vitro by Synbio Technologies Co., Ltd. (Suzhou, China), as shown in Fig. S2. DNA sequences listed in Table S1 were synthesized and modified by Shanghai Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 10 μg/mL microcystins (MC-LR, microcystin-RR (MC-RR), microcystin-YR (MC-YR), and microcystin-LW (MC-LW)) standard solutions were obtained from Eurofins Abraxis, Inc. (Warminster, PA, USA). Fe(III) meso-tetra(4-carboxyphenyl)porphine chloride (TCPP(Fe)) was purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 4 S GelBlue nucleic acid dye was obtained from Shanghai Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Other chemicals were all acquired from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

The Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (F97Pro, Lengguang Co., Ltd., China) and UV-Vis spectrophotometer (T9CS, Persee Co., Ltd., China) were used to record the fluorescence spectra and UV-Vis absorption spectra, respectively. Scanning electron microscope (SEM, ZEISS MERLIN Compact, ZEISS, Germany), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Titan G260–300, FEI, USA), atomic force microscopy (AFM, Dimension ICON, Bruker, Germany), diffractometer (XRD, D8 Advance, Bruker, Germany), thermal gravimetric analyzer (TG, TGA 5500, TA Instruments, Inc., USA), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy apparatus (XPS, EscaLab Xi+, Thermo Fisher, USA), Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet is50, Thermo Fisher, USA), and zeta potential analyzer (Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK) were all performed to characterize the synthesized materials. The electrophoresis apparatus (JY-TD331A, Beijing Junyi-Dongfang Electrophoresis Equipment Co., Ltd., China) and Bioanalytical Imaging System (Azure 600, Azure Biosysterms Inc., USA) were used for electrophoresis analysis.

Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheet, one kind of 2D-pMOFs, was employed as the fluorescence quencher in this study. The Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets were prepared according to the previously reported method with some modifications (Zhao et al., 2015). Briefly, 0.005 mmol TCPP(Fe) in 4 mL DMF/ethanol mixture (3:1) was added dropwise into a 12 mL DMF/ethanol mixture solution (3:1) containing 0.01 mmol Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, 10 µL trifluoroacetic acid and 10.0 mg polyvinylpyrrolidone under stirring. After 10 min of ultrasonication, the above mixture was transferred to the reactor and heated at 80 ℃ for 15 h. The dark brown products were collected by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 10 min) after washing twice with ethanol. Finally, the obtained Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets were stored in ethanol for further use.

To prepare fluorescence reporter, 400 nM ssDNA-FAM was mixed with 30 µg/mL Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets in a 1× buffer A (25 mM NaCl, 20 mM HEPES, 1 mM DTT, 5% glycerol and 5 mM MgCl2, pH 7.5) solution at room temperature for 10 min. In the meanwhile, 250 nM Cas14a was incubated with 150 nM sgRNA in 1× buffer A solution at 37 °C for 15 min to assemble the Cas14a-sgRNA complex. Then, 3 µL cDNA (activator of Cas14a) with different concentrations and 10 µL reporter were added into the solution subsequently. The total volume of the reaction solution was 20 µL. The reaction was carried out at 37 °C with continuous shaking for up to 210 min. Finally, DEPC water was added to give a final volume of 100 µL, and the reaction solution was measured by a fluorescence spectrometer (λex: 490 nm, λem: 510–600 nm).

A competitive reaction between cDNA and MC-LR with MC-LR aptamer was carried out in a 1× buffer B (50 mM Tris-HCl, 150 mM NaCl and 2 mM MgCl2, pH 7.5) solution. Initially, MC-LR with different concentrations was incubated with 100 nM MC-LR aptamer solution at 25 °C for 30 min, followed by the addition of 120 nM cDNA into the solution. The mixture solution was incubated at 25 °C for another 10 min. Then, 3 µL competitive reaction solution and 10 µL fluorescence reporter were added into Cas14a-sgRNA solution (250 nM Cas14a and 150 nM sgRNA in 1× buffer A). The solution was incubated at 37 °C for 210 min with continuous shaking. After that, additional DEPC water was added into the solution to give a final volume of 100 µL, and the reaction solution was measured via a fluorescence spectrometer (λex: 490 nm, λem: 510–600 nm).

To further confirm the feasibility of the Cas14-pMOFs sensor and its application in MC-LR detection, the reactants and products were analyzed with 8% native polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) in a 1× TBE running buffer (90 mM Tris-HCl, 2 mM EDTA, 90 mM boric acid, pH 7.8) solution at 120 V for 70 min. The gels were stained by 4S GelBlue nucleic acid dye for 20–30 min and were imaged using the bioanalytical imaging system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Working principle of the Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor for MC-LR determination
ce:section.ce:para: The combination of the CRISPR/Cas14a system and 2D-pMOFs enabled the sensitive detection of MC-LR without nucleic acid amplification and complicated instruments ( Scheme 1). On the one hand, a 2D porphyrin metal-organic framework nanosheet, Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheet was utilized to absorb the FAM-labeled ssDNA (ssDNA-FAM) to prepare the fluorescence reporter (Cu-TCPP(Fe)/ssDNA-FAM), at this time, the fluorescence of ssDNA-FAM was quenched by Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets (Scheme 1A). On the other hand, cDNA was designed to be complementary to MC-LR aptamer and sgRNA. When MC-LR was added into the system, the MC-LR was competitively bound to its aptamer because of the high binding affinity between MC-LR and aptamer. Thus, the cDNA was not able to bind the aptamer and was freely distributed in the solution. As a result, freely distributed cDNA bind to the sgRNA to activate the collateral cleavage activity of Cas14a. The ssDNA-FAM attached on the Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets were cut into DNA fragments by the activated Cas14a, which were detached from the Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets and emitted fluorescence signal (Scheme 1B). On the contrary, the cDNA and MC-LR aptamer would be hybridized to form a duplex in the absence of MC-LR, and Cas14a can not be activated, resulting in no fluorescence signal. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity in this system was positively correlated with the amount of the activated Cas14a, which in turn positively depend on the concentration of the MC-LR.

We prepared Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets as fluorescence adsorption and quencher substrate. The morphology of Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets was obtained using SEM, TEM and AFM, respectively. SEM images showed that a typical sheet-like structure of Cu-TCPP(Fe) was stacked together, owing to the thinness of the Cu-TCPP(Fe) structure ( Figs. 1A and S3). The low contrast of Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets in TEM images indicated their ultrathin feature, and the elemental mapping proved the uniform distribution of C, N, O, Cu, and Fe elements (Figs. 1B-D and S4). Additionally, the AFM further confirmed the ultrathin nature of the Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets with an average thickness of 3.4 ± 0.8 nm (Fig. 1E-F). The peaks of (110), (200), (004), (400), (330) and (440) in the XRD pattern indicated the crystalline structure of Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets (Fig. S5) (Zuo et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2021). These results manifested the successful preparation of ultrathin 2D Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets. Moreover, the TG analysis showed two main weight loss steps (Fig. S6). The first weight loss was about 13% from 30 to 350 ℃, corresponding to the loss of solvents. The second weight loss was about 70% from 350 to 600 ℃, which was attributed to the decomposition of Cu-TCPP(Fe) (Golpour and Pakizeh, 2018). The TG result confirmed the good thermal stability of the Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets. The concentration of Cu2+ and Fe3+ were undetectable in the supernatant after 10 days of storage, suggesting the good storage stability of the Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets (Table S2).

To further examine the chemical structure of the Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets, the XPS spectra were recorded ( Fig. 2). The XPS survey spectrum confirmed the existence of C, N, O, Cu and Fe elements, and the peaks correspond to C1s, N1s, O1s, Cu2p3, Cu2p1, Fe2p3, and Fe2p1, respectively (Fig. 2A), which were consistent with the TEM elemental mapping. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, O1s, Cu2p, and Fe2p were analyzed. The high-resolution C1s spectra showed the fitted peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.4 eV and 288.2 eV, which were ascribed to C-C, C-N and CO/C-O, respectively (Fig. 2B) (Cheng et al., 2021). The N1s spectra had two peaks centered at 398.4 eV and 399.5 eV, which were contributed to C-N and N-Fe, respectively (Fig. 2C). The N-Fe motif indicated the metalation of porphyrin ring with Fe in TCPP(Fe) (Zhang et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2018). In the high-resolution O1s spectra, the peaks at 531.7 eV and 533.7 eV were assigned to CO and O-H, respectively (Fig. 2D). The high-resolution spectra signals of Cu2p appeared at 935.1 eV and 955.3 eV for Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2, respectively (Fig. 2E), while the high-resolution Fe2p spectra revealed the signals of the Fe2p3/2 (711.3 eV) and Fe2p1/2 (723.6 eV) of Fe3+ (Fig. 2F) (Cheng et al., 2021; Li et al., 2018). The FTIR results for the TCPP(Fe) and Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets were presented in Fig. S7. It showed that the peak at 1688 cm−1, which was related to CO stretching vibration in -COOH groups, was shifted and significantly weakened (Cheng et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2020). Two new peaks at 1611 cm−1 and 1403 cm−1 corresponding to the C-O-Cu stretching were appeared, confirming the successful coordination between the Cu2+ and carboxyl ligand of TCPP(Fe). The peak at about 1000 cm−1 related to the Fe-N stretching vibration showed no obvious differences in TCPP(Fe) and Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets, suggesting iron-porphyrin structure is intact after the formation of Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets (Shi et al., 2018). Based on the analysis of XPS and FTIR spectra, the synthetic route and chemical structure of the Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets was illustrated in Fig. S8.

The π-electron system in the Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets makes it easy for the self-assembly of ssDNA and Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets (Zhao et al., 2015). The zeta potential before and after the addition of ssDNA into the Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets solution were measured. As shown in Fig. 3A, the zeta potential of Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets (−9.14 +0.35 mV) shifted to a more negative value (−10.06 +0.44 mV) after the addition of ssDNA into the Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets solution. This result indicated the ssDNA was attached onto the Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets, because the backbone of FAM-labeled ssDNA is negatively charged (Liu et al., 2020).

The fluorescence quenching ability of the Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets was studied. As shown in Fig. 3B, before the mixture, the ssDNA-FAM, with a quantum yield of 0.70, showed a strong emission, while the Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets exhibited no emission. After mixing the ssDNA-FAM with Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets, the fluorescence intensity was significantly quenched, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets on ssDNA-FAM was about 99%, and the quenching occurred rapidly within 10 min, demonstrating the excellent fluorescence quenching ability of Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets. Moreover, compared with other MOFs or commonly used quenching nanomaterials, Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets exhibited much better quenching efficiency and faster quenching kinetics (Fig. S9), suggesting that the Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets can be utilized as an ideal fluorescence quencher for fluorescence sensors.

We further explored the quenching mechanism of Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets to ssDNA-FAM. Fig. S10A-B showed that the fluorescence intensities of ssDNA-FAM decreased with increasing Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets concentrations. The changes of fluorescence intensity fitted well with the Lineweaver-Burk equation (Fig. S10C), but fitted poorly with the Stern-Volmer equation (Fig. S10D), indicating the static quenching was the main contributor (He et al., 2008). The UV–visible absorption spectra showed a hypochromicity with the addition of Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets (Fig. S10E), suggesting the ground-state complex was formed between Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets and ssDNA-FAM, because the ground-state complex could result in the change of the absorption spectrum of the fluorophore, which further confirmed the static quenching mechanism of ssDNA-FAM by the Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets (He et al., 2008). Considering that the Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets had a wide absorption in the visible light region and there was an obvious spectral overlap between Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets absorption and ssDNA-FAM emission (Fig. S10F), the fluorescence quenching may due to the FRET from excited fluorophore to 2D-MOF (Zu et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of the Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The collateral cleavage activity of Cas14a and signal output of Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets-based fluorescence reporter are two key components for this Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor. We initially utilized cDNA as the target to verify the feasibility of this sensor. As can be seen from Fig. 4A, no obvious fluorescence characteristic peak was observed for the reporter, cDNA+Cas14a+reporter, cDNA+sgRNA+reporter, and Cas14a+sgRNA+reporter, respectively. However, the collateral cleavage activity of Cas14a was activated by the addition of cDNA in the presence of both sgRNA and reporter, as reflected by the strong fluorescence characteristic peak (red curve in Fig. 4A) in the cDNA+Cas14a+sgRNA+reporter mixture solution. To verify the results obtained from the fluorescence studies, the native polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) was performed. As shown in Fig. 4B, an obvious band between 20 and 40 bp representing MOF/h-ssDNA reporter was observed in L1. This band showed no obvious change in L2 and L3 because the Cas-sgRNA complex was not formed in the absence of Cas14a or sgRNA. Similarly, no change was observed in the reporter band in the absence of cDNA because the CRISPR/Cas system was not activated without target DNA even though the Cas-sgRNA complex was formed. The above results showed no cleavage effect occurred in the absence of cDNA, Cas14a, and sgRNA. The band between 20 and 40 bp in L5 was darker than that in L1-L4 bands, indicating that the h-ssDNA was cleaved into short fragments when cDNA was employed as the activator for CRISPR/Cas14a system. It well signifies that the Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor can be used in the quantification of cDNA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve a good analytical performance of the Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor, the important factors, such as the concentration of Cas14, the amount of reporter, the cleavage time, and the pH were optimized. From Fig. S11A, we observed that the fluorescence difference (△F) became more significant with increasing Cas14a concentrations, and reached a plateau when the concentration of Cas14a was above 250 nM. We then optimized the concentration of the reporter (expressed as Cu-TCPP(Fe) concentration), as exhibited in Fig. S11B. The most significant difference in △F was observed when the concentration of the reporter was 15 µg/mL. This phenomenon was rationalized as follows: when the concentration of Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets was low, the excessive ssDNA-FAM could not be fully absorbed on the Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets, so the fluorescence of ssDNA-FAM could not be efficiently quenched, resulting in a high fluorescence background. However, when the concentration of Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets was high, the excess amount of Cu-TCPP(Fe) nanosheets that existed in the solution increased steric hindrance, the inefficient cleavage of ssDNA-FAM using activated Cas14a was observed. The cleavage time of the assay was optimized and the results were presented in Fig. S11C. The △F increased with the increments of the cleavage time, and the highest △F was obtained when the time was 210 min. Slightly decrease of the △F was observed when the time was over 210 min, probably due to the fluorescence decay. The pH was optimized to be 7.5, as shown in Fig. S11D. We thus optimized the working conditions for the Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor as follows: the concentration of Cas14 was 250 nM, the amount of reporter was 15 µg/mL, the cleavage time was 210 min and pH was 7.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor for DNA determination
ce:section.ce:para:
The sensitive and accurate detection of cDNA by Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor is the basis for its usage in MC-LR detection, since the cDNA concentrations were positively correlated with the MC-LR concentrations. As shown in Fig. 5A-B, the fluorescence signal increased with increasing target cDNA concentrations (C
cDNA ). A good linear relationship ranging from 1.2 nM to 120 nM was obtained with the linear equations of △F = 20.7 C cDNA + 572.3 (R2 = 0.93) (Fig. 5C). The LOD was calculated to be 0.12 nM by interpolating the mean value plus three standard deviations of blank samples (n = 6). To verify the specificity of DNA target-dependent fluorescence signal using this Cas14-pMOF fluorescence sensor, mismatched targets, including 2-nt mismatches (M2), 4-nt mismatches (M4), 6-nt mismatches (M6), and 8-nt mismatches (M8) were selected, respectively. As exhibited in Fig. 5D, after DNA was added into this Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor, only exactly matched DNA induced high fluorescence intensity while the mismatched DNAs showed much lower fluorescence intensities. It proved that the Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor showed a good specificity in the detection of nucleic acid targets. These results ensured the following application of utilizing the Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor for MC-LR detection after introducing functional DNA molecules such as aptamers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor for MC-LR detection
ce:section.ce:para: With the assistance of the above convincing results, the Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor was further utilized for the MC-LR detection. The fluorescence signals of the solutions with different components were measured, as shown in Fig. 6A. It can be seen that the reporter, the aptamer+reporter, cDNA+reporter, MC-LR+reporter, and aptamer+cDNA+reporter showed almost no fluorescence. The aptamer+cDNA+Cas14a/sgRNA+reporter showed a high fluorescence even though no MC-LR was added. This is because the excess amount of cDNA was added into the solution, the unbound cDNA activated the Cas14a, resulting in a strong fluorescence signal. However, a higher fluorescence signal at 520 nm was observed in solutions containing MC-LR+aptamer+cDNA+Cas14a/sgRNA+reporter, because more cDNA was available to activate the Cas14a after the addition of MC-LR into the solution. The results indicate that the MC-LR could be detected through the homogeneous competition reaction between cDNA and MC-LR with the aptamer and Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence system. We further confirmed the fluorescence results with PAGE, as shown in Fig. 6B. The bright band in L5 at ca. 100 bp appeared, which indicates the formation of the hybrid aptamer/cDNA duplex. When adding the aptamer/cDNA duplex into Cas14-pMOFs (L6), the band ascribed to MOF/h-ssDNA remained almost unchanged compared to that obtained in L1-L5, due to the limited amounts of activated Cas14a in solutions. However, this band in L7 was almost invisible when MC-LR was added, suggesting the cleavage of MOF/h-ssDNA occurred with Cas14a, which was activated by a sufficient amount of free cDNA presented in the solution through the competitive homogeneous reaction process.

Before the exploration of the analytical performance of the Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor for MC-LR detection, the concentration ratio of MC-LR aptamer to cDNA (100:120) was optimized (Fig. S12) to obtain the highest △F. Then, the analytical performance of the Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor for MC-LR detection was studied. The fluorescence spectra in the presence of different concentrations of MC-LR were obtained as shown in Fig. 7A. The fluorescence signal change (△F) increased linearly with increasing C
MC-LR in the range from 50 pg/mL to 3 ng/mL and increased with a smaller slope in the range beyond 3 ng/mL (Fig. 7B). The △F was linearly related to the logarithm of C
MC-LR in the range of 50 pg/mL to1 μg/mL and the linear equation was △F = 254.1 logC
MC-LR + 615.1 (R2 = 0.99) with a LOD of 19 pg/mL (based on the mean value plus three standard deviations of blank samples (n = 6)) (Fig. 7C), demonstrating that the Cas14-pMOFs had a comparable linear range and LOD for MC-LR detection, compared with the other developed detection methods ( Table 1). And this Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor was more than adequate for MC-LR determination as the maximum limit for MC-LR of 1 μg/L in drinking water was proposed by the World Health Organization (Yang et al., 2020). Although, as an emerging technology, the Cas protein-based method is more expensive than some other established ones (e.g., HPLC and ELISA). This situation will be largely alleviated as the Cas14a protein expression is industrialized for bigger markets.

To assess the selectivity of the Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor in MC-LR detection, the same fluorescence assay was utilized to determine the relative fluorescence intensity of the three different structural analogs (MC-YR, MC-RR, and MC-LW) in the microcystins family. As displayed in Fig. 7D, compared to these analogs, the strongest △F was observed for MC-LR with the corresponding concentrations of 1 ng/mL, 10 ng/mL, and 100 ng/mL, respectively, suggesting a high selectivity of this assay toward MC-LR. Notice here that, even though these three different analogs of MC-LR showed appreciable fluorescence signal, especially for MC-YR and MC-RR, probably owing to the moderate binding affinity between the MC-YR and MC-RR with the aptamer (Ng et al., 2012), this situation can be largely alleviated by developing a new MC-LR aptamer, which can bind to the MC-LR more specifically.

Lastly, we validated the feasibility and reliability of the Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor for the detection of MC-LR in actual samples. The standard recovery experiments were applied in drink water and tap water spiked with different concentrations of MC-LR. The experimental results were shown in Table 2. The average recoveries of tap water and drink water were in the range of 71.5–96.2%, with the relative standard deviations (RSDs) within 3.2–18.5%. These results indicated that the Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor could be applied for the determination of MC-LR in drinking water and tap water.

In summary, we developed a novel and universal Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor by integrating the CRISPR/Cas14a system with the 2D porphyrin metal organic-framework nanosheets. By introducing the homogeneous competitive reaction between MC-LR and cDNA with aptamer, we further applied this novel sensor for the sensitive determination of MC-LR with a LOD of 19 pg/mL. The results confirmed the feasibility and suitability of the Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor as an advantageous method for MC-LR detection, and expanded the applications of the CRISPR/Cas14a-based analytical method for the detection of non-nucleic acid targets. In addition, this developed Cas14-pMOFs fluorescence sensor shows the advantage in versatility, as the system does not restrict to DNA target sequence and can be used for the detection of other important targets by simply redesigning the sgRNA.
